text:
Aussie B-Girl Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn has retaliated at scrutinisers in a fiery post amid widespread mockery over her disaster performance in breaking's debut at the Paris Olympic Games.

An Australian breaking athlete has hit back at social media users after she spun headfirst into rampant scrutiny over a disaster performance in the dance’s debut at the 2024 Paris Olympic Games.

B-Girl Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn, 36, on Friday lost all three pool matches against the USA, France and Lithuania in the sport’s opener at Paris’ Place de la Concorde.

Fans on social media slammed the Australian representative for her clumsy roll and wriggle on the floor resulting in the nation losing to Japan’s gold medal B-Girl Ami.

“Reminds me of when my dog has an ear infection and has to rub its head in the lawn,” one person said on X of Raygun’s performance.

“As an Australian, I 100% apologise for this embarrassment,” said another.

“I have nothing funny to say about Rachael 'Raygun' Gunn. Her disgraceful performance has brought a great shame upon our nation,” said another.

A fourth person likened the athletes awkward act to “6-year-olds after saying ‘watch this’”.

Enthusiasts also mocked Gunn’s Australian Olympic tracksuit as her choice of uniform, a stark contrast to her competitor’s hip streetwear styles.

The 36-year-old came head-to-head with the backlash on Saturday and took to Instagram stories with a fiery shot at her tormenters.

“Looking forward to the same level of scrutiny on what the bboys wear tomorrow,” she wrote over a yellow, green and blue gradient backdrop.

Raygun earlier shared a post from the Australian breaking team in which she could be heard ranting about the importance of individuality.

“Don’t be afraid to be different,” she said.

“Go out there and represent yourself, you never know where that’s gonna take you.”

Gunn glared down the camera and made a performative gesture with her hat before leaping out to eager crowds waiting for the Australian PHD candidate and university lecturer to make move in France’s capital.

The dancer is Australia's top-ranking breakdancer and a creative arts researcher specialising in the cultural politics of breakdancing at Sydney's Macquarie University.

She secured her place in the Games as Australia’s b-girl delegate after winning last year’s Oceania Breaking Championships against a dancer named Molly.

Speaking to reporters after her Paris flop failed to maintain her status as a breaking great, Gunn admitted she was never going to win in 2024.

“All my moves are original,” she said.

“I was never going to beat these girls on what they do best, the dynamic and the power moves, so I wanted to move differently, be artistic and creative because how many chances do you get in a lifetime to do that on an international stage?

“I was always the underdog and wanted to make my mark in a different way.”

“I know how rare this opportunity is and I wanted to take the chance to wear the green and gold,” she said.

“I don’t know why some of the other girls didn’t want to wear their kit. It was a real moment of pride for me to wear the Australian uniform, I was particularly excited for this shirt to have the Indigenous print on the arms.”

“After I’ve recovered, I’ll probably be writing about it.”

Sympathetic fans flooded social media with messages of support for the Australian.

“Props to Raygun for having the guts to go out there,” one person said.

“Raygun from Australia is my Olympic hero actually,” another added.

Breaking featured for the first time on the main Olympic stage and was welcomed with a booming introduction from US rapper Snoop Dogg.

It has faced much controversy and will not return for the 2028 Games in Los Angeles.

The sport will have one more moment in the spotlight on Sunday with Australia’s 16-year-old B-boy Jeff (J Attack) Dunne set to front in the male competition at 12.17am.

title: Aussie B-Girl Rachael ‘Raygun’ Gunn, 36, retaliates at scrutinisers in fiery social media post after 'embarrassing' breaking debut at Paris Olympics spins mockery | Sky News Australia
description: An Australian breaking athlete has hit back at social media users after she spun headfirst into rampant scrutiny over a disaster performance in the dance’s debut at the 2024 Paris Olympic Games.
